expanded_description: The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem states that the sum of the measures of the three interior angles of any triangle is always exactly 180°. The Exterior Angle Theorem states that the measure of an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the measures of the two remote interior angles. These two theorems are among the most fundamental and frequently used results in all of geometry, serving as the foundation for polygon angle sums, triangle congruence proofs, and countless problem-solving applications.

core_concept.principle: Every triangle, regardless of its shape or size, has interior angles that sum to 180°. An exterior angle—formed by extending one side of the triangle—equals the sum of the two interior angles that are not adjacent to it (the remote interior angles). The exterior angle is always greater than either remote interior angle alone. These relationships are provable using parallel line postulates and are the logical basis for all polygon angle formulas.
core_concept.explanation: Think of the Triangle Angle Sum as a fixed budget: every triangle gets exactly 180° to distribute among its three angles. If one angle is big, the other two must be small. If two angles are equal, the third is determined. The Exterior Angle Theorem is like a shortcut: instead of measuring an exterior angle directly, just add the two opposite interior angles. This is because the exterior angle and its adjacent interior angle form a linear pair (180°), and all three interior angles sum to 180°, so the exterior must equal the sum of the other two. These two theorems turn angle chasing—figuring out unknown angles in complex diagrams—from guesswork into systematic deduction.

image.alt: Two panels: (1) A triangle with all three interior angles labeled $\angle A$, $\angle B$, $\angle C$. Equation: $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$. The angles are shown being 'folded' to form a straight line. (2) A triangle with one side extended, forming exterior angle $\angle 1$. The two remote interior angles are highlighted. Equation: $m\angle 1 = m\angle A + m\angle B$. The exterior angle is shown larger than either remote angle.
image.caption: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem: $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$. Exterior Angle Theorem: exterior = sum of two remote interior angles.

sections.introduction: The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem is one of the oldest and most important results in geometry. It states that the three interior angles of any triangle always sum to exactly 180°. This theorem is proved using parallel line postulates and is the foundation for calculating unknown angles, proving triangle congruence, and deriving formulas for all polygons.

introduction_image.alt: Three different triangles—acute, right, and obtuse—each with interior angles labeled and summed to 180°. Acute: $50°+60°+70°=180°$. Right: $30°+60°+90°=180°$. Obtuse: $25°+35°+120°=180°$. A visual 'proof' shows the three angles torn off and arranged to form a straight line.
introduction_image.caption: The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem: all triangles, regardless of shape, have interior angles summing to 180°.

key_concept.definition: The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem: The sum of the measures of the interior angles of any triangle is 180°. Symbolically: $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$. This holds for ALL triangles—scalene, isosceles, equilateral, acute, right, and obtuse.
key_concept.context: This theorem is used constantly in geometry: to find a missing angle when two are known, to prove triangles are congruent or similar, to derive the formula for the sum of interior angles of any polygon $(n-2) \times 180°$, and to establish the Exterior Angle Theorem. It is one of the most frequently applied theorems in all of mathematics.

image.alt: A triangle $\triangle ABC$ with angles labeled $A$, $B$, $C$. The equation $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$ displayed prominently. A proof sketch shown: line through $C$ parallel to $AB$ creates alternate interior angles equal to $\angle A$ and $\angle B$, forming a straight angle of 180° at $C$.
image.caption: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem: $A + B + C = 180°$. Proved using parallel lines and alternate interior angles.

process_flow.description: Determine which two (or all three) angles of the triangle are known from given information, the diagram, or previous calculations.
process_flow.mechanism: Read angle measures from given values, marked angles, or algebraic expressions. Label the triangle with the known measures. If only one angle is known and the triangle is isosceles or right, use those properties to find a second angle before applying the theorem.
process_flow.result: At least two known angle measures (or algebraic expressions for them).
applications: Given a right triangle with one acute angle $= 35°$ → the right angle is $90°$.
Given an isosceles triangle with vertex angle $40°$ → base angles are equal.

image.alt: A triangle with two angles labeled: $\angle A = 45°$, $\angle B = 75°$. The third angle $\angle C$ marked with a question mark. The known values are highlighted.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the known angles in the triangle.

process_flow.description: Set up the equation $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$. Substitute known values and solve for the unknown angle.
process_flow.mechanism: Add the known angle measures. Subtract this sum from $180°$ to find the missing angle. If angles are given as algebraic expressions, set up the equation and solve for the variable first, then substitute back to find each angle measure.
process_flow.result: The measure of the unknown angle, or the value of the variable.
applications: For a right triangle: $90° + 35° + m\angle B = 180°$ → $m\angle B = 55°$.

image.alt: The equation $45° + 75° + m\angle C = 180°$ written out. Simplification shown: $120° + m\angle C = 180°$ → $m\angle C = 60°$. The triangle now has all three angles labeled.
image.caption: Step 2: Set up and solve $A + B + C = 180°$.

process_flow.description: Check that the sum is indeed 180° and that the resulting angles make sense for the triangle's classification.
process_flow.mechanism: Add all three angles—they must total exactly $180°$. Verify that each angle is positive and less than $180°$. If the triangle is classified by angles, check consistency: acute triangle (all $< 90°$), right triangle (one $= 90°$), obtuse triangle (one $> 90°$).
process_flow.result: A verified, consistent set of angle measures for the triangle.
applications: If an angle is negative or $\geq 180°$, an error was made.

image.alt: The triangle with all three angles displayed: 45°, 75°, 60°. A checkmark confirms $45°+75°+60°=180°$. Classification: 'Acute triangle — all angles < 90°.'
image.caption: Step 3: Verify the sum is 180° and the triangle's angle classification is consistent.

classifications.value: The Angle Sum Theorem is frequently used to set up and solve equations when angle measures involve variables.
characteristics: Angles expressed as algebraic expressions: e.g., $(2x+10)°$, $(3x-5)°$, $(x+25)°$
Set up equation: expression1 + expression2 + expression3 = 180°
Solve for the variable, then substitute back to find each angle measure
Always check that each resulting angle is positive and less than 180°
classifications.behavior: This is one of the most common problem types in geometry. The algebra is straightforward—combine like terms and solve a linear equation. The geometric context provides the equation.

examples.process: Solving for x and finding angles

image.alt: Triangle with algebraic angle expressions. Equation setup: $2x + (3x+10) + (x+30) = 180°$. Step-by-step solution. Final angle values labeled on the triangle. Verification sum shown.
image.caption: Algebraic Angle Sum: set up the equation, solve for the variable, find all angles.

image.alt: Complete section summary: the Angle Sum Theorem statement, the three-step process for finding unknown angles, algebraic application example, and the geometric proof using parallel lines.
image.caption: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem overview: statement, applications, and proof.

sections.introduction: An exterior angle of a triangle is formed by extending one side of the triangle. At each vertex, there are two possible exterior angles (they are vertical angles and therefore congruent). The Exterior Angle Theorem states that the measure of an exterior angle equals the sum of the measures of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles. This theorem provides a powerful shortcut for finding angle measures and establishing inequalities.

introduction_image.alt: A triangle $\triangle ABC$ with side $BC$ extended to point $D$, forming exterior angle $\angle ACD$. The two remote interior angles $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ are highlighted in a different color from the adjacent interior angle $\angle C$. Equation: $m\angle ACD = m\angle A + m\angle B$. The exterior angle is visually larger than either $\angle A$ or $\angle B$ alone.
introduction_image.caption: Exterior Angle Theorem: exterior angle = sum of the two remote interior angles.

key_concept.definition: The Exterior Angle Theorem: The measure of an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the measures of the two remote interior angles (the angles not adjacent to the exterior angle). Symbolically: $m\angle 1 = m\angle A + m\angle B$, where $\angle 1$ is the exterior angle and $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ are the remote interior angles. Corollary: The exterior angle is greater than either remote interior angle alone ($m\angle 1 > m\angle A$ and $m\angle 1 > m\angle B$).
key_concept.context: This theorem is proved from the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem and the Linear Pair Postulate. It is used to find exterior angles from interior angles, to find interior angles when an exterior angle is known, to prove inequalities in triangles, and to analyze polygons where exterior angles play a key role.

image.alt: Complete diagram: $\triangle ABC$ with $BC$ extended to $D$. Exterior angle $\angle ACD$ labeled. Remote interior angles $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ highlighted. Adjacent interior angle $\angle C$ shown forming a linear pair with $\angle ACD$. Proof equations: $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$ and $m\angle ACD + m\angle C = 180°$, therefore $m\angle ACD = m\angle A + m\angle B$.
image.caption: Exterior Angle Theorem: $m\angle ACD = m\angle A + m\angle B$. Exterior > either remote interior angle.

process_flow.process: Identify the Exterior Angle and Remote Interior Angles
process_flow.description: Locate the exterior angle (formed by extending a side) and identify the two remote interior angles—the angles of the triangle that are NOT adjacent to the exterior angle.
process_flow.mechanism: An exterior angle is formed by one side of the triangle and the extension of an adjacent side. The interior angle adjacent to the exterior angle forms a linear pair with it. The other two interior angles are the remote interior angles—they are 'across' the triangle from the exterior angle.
process_flow.result: Correct identification of the exterior angle and its two remote interior angles.
applications: Extend side $BC$ past $C$ to point $D$. Exterior angle: $\angle ACD$. Remote interiors: $\angle A$ and $\angle B$.
Extend side $AB$ past $B$ to point $E$. Exterior angle: $\angle CBE$. Remote interiors: $\angle A$ and $\angle C$.
The exterior angle and its adjacent interior angle sum to 180° (linear pair).

image.alt: Triangle with one side extended. The exterior angle is highlighted with an arc. Two remote interior angles are circled and labeled. The adjacent interior angle is shown with a linear pair symbol (180°) with the exterior angle.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the exterior angle and its two remote interior angles.

process_flow.description: Write the equation: $m\angle_{\text{exterior}} = m\angle_{\text{remote1}} + m\angle_{\text{remote2}}$. Substitute known values and solve.
process_flow.mechanism: If the two remote interior angles are known, add them to find the exterior angle. If the exterior angle and one remote interior angle are known, subtract to find the other remote interior angle. If expressions are given, set up the equation and solve for the variable.
process_flow.result: The unknown angle measure, found without needing to use the full Angle Sum Theorem.
applications: Exterior $= 5x + 10$, remotes $= 2x$ and $3x - 5$ → $5x + 10 = 5x - 5$? That wouldn't work. Let's use: exterior $= 7x$, remotes $= 3x$ and $2x + 10$ → $7x = 5x + 10$ → $2x = 10$ → $x = 5$.

image.alt: The equation $m\angle ACD = m\angle A + m\angle B$ written with specific values: $m\angle ACD = 40° + 60° = 100°$. The exterior angle is labeled 100°. Verification: adjacent interior + 100° = 180°.
image.caption: Step 2: Apply the theorem — exterior = remote + remote.

classifications.value: A corollary of the Exterior Angle Theorem: the exterior angle is always greater than either remote interior angle.
characteristics: Since the exterior equals the SUM of the two remotes, it must be larger than each individually
This inequality is used in triangle inequality proofs and to order angles by size
The exterior angle is NOT necessarily greater than the adjacent interior angle
classifications.behavior: This inequality is intuitive—the whole is greater than any of its parts. The exterior angle 'absorbs' both remote angles, making it automatically larger than either one alone. It is NOT necessarily larger than the adjacent interior angle (which can be obtuse while the exterior is acute).

examples.process: Exterior angle vs. remote interior angles
examples.explanation:
Triangle with angles $30°$, $50°$, $100°$.

Exterior angle at the vertex of the $100°$ angle: equals $30° + 50° = 80°$.

Here $80° < 100°$ — the exterior angle is LESS than the adjacent interior angle!

But $80° > 30°$ and $80° > 50°$ — the exterior IS greater than both remote interior angles.

image.alt: Triangle with angles 30°, 50°, 100°. Exterior angle at the 100° vertex is 80° (adjacent to 100°). Arrows show: 80° > 30° and 80° > 50° (greater than both remotes), but 80° < 100° (not greater than adjacent).
image.caption: Exterior angle > each remote interior angle. NOT necessarily > adjacent interior angle.

image.alt: Complete section summary: the Exterior Angle Theorem statement, identification guide, application examples, and the exterior angle inequality corollary.
image.caption: Exterior Angle Theorem overview: definition, applications, and the inequality corollary.

sections.introduction: The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem and the Exterior Angle Theorem are closely related. In fact, the Exterior Angle Theorem is a direct logical consequence of the Angle Sum Theorem combined with the Linear Pair Postulate. Understanding this connection provides deeper insight into both theorems and offers multiple solution paths for finding unknown angles.

introduction_image.alt: A triangle with one side extended. Two solution paths shown: Path 1 (Angle Sum) — find third interior angle, then use linear pair to get exterior. Path 2 (Exterior Angle Theorem) — add the two remote interior angles directly. Both paths arrive at the same exterior angle measure. The equivalence of the paths is highlighted.
introduction_image.caption: Two paths to the same answer: the Exterior Angle Theorem is a shortcut derived from the Angle Sum Theorem.

key_concept.definition: The Exterior Angle Theorem is proved using the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem and the Linear Pair Postulate. Given $\triangle ABC$ with exterior angle $\angle ACD$: (1) $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$ (Angle Sum). (2) $m\angle ACD + m\angle C = 180°$ (Linear Pair). Equating the two expressions for $180°$: $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = m\angle ACD + m\angle C$. Subtract $m\angle C$: $m\angle A + m\angle B = m\angle ACD$. This proves the theorem.
key_concept.context: This connection means any problem that can be solved with the Exterior Angle Theorem can also be solved with the Angle Sum Theorem plus Linear Pair—the exterior angle theorem is simply a more efficient path. Recognizing both approaches provides flexibility in problem-solving.

image.alt: The proof of the Exterior Angle Theorem displayed: (1) $A+B+C=180°$, (2) Exterior $+ C = 180°$, (3) Set equal: $A+B+C =$ Exterior $+ C$, (4) Subtract $C$: $A+B =$ Exterior. The logical flow is diagrammed with the triangle.
image.caption: Proof of the Exterior Angle Theorem from the Angle Sum Theorem and Linear Pair Postulate.

worked_examples.title: Using Both Theorems Together
worked_examples.problem:
Solve each problem using both theorems where appropriate.

1) In $\triangle ABC$, $m\angle A = 50°$ and $m\angle B = 70°$. Find $m\angle C$ and the exterior angle at vertex $C$.
2) The exterior angle at vertex $P$ of $\triangle PQR$ measures $130°$. One remote interior angle measures $55°$. Find all three interior angles.
3) In $\triangle XYZ$, $m\angle X = 3x - 10$, $m\angle Y = 2x + 20$, and the exterior angle at $Z$ is $6x - 30$. Find $x$ and all interior angles.

step_by_step.explanation:
Angle Sum: $50° + 70° + m\angle C = 180°$ → $m\angle C = 60°$.

Exterior at $C$ (using Linear Pair): $180° - 60° = 120°$.

Or (using Exterior Angle Theorem): $m\angle A + m\angle B = 50° + 70° = 120°$.

Both methods give $120°$.

Exterior at $P = 130°$ = sum of remote interiors $\angle Q$ and $\angle R$.

Given one remote $= 55°$ → other remote $= 130° - 55° = 75°$.

Adjacent interior $\angle P = 180° - 130° = 50°$ (Linear Pair).

Interior angles: $50°$, $55°$, $75°$. Check: $50°+55°+75°=180°$ ✓.

Exterior at $Z$ equals sum of remote interiors $\angle X$ and $\angle Y$.

$6x - 30 = (3x - 10) + (2x + 20)$
$6x - 30 = 5x + 10$
$x = 40$

$m\angle X = 3(40) - 10 = 110°$
$m\angle Y = 2(40) + 20 = 100°$
Wait — $110° + 100° = 210° > 180°$, which is impossible for two angles of a triangle. Something's wrong. Let me check: $m\angle X + m\angle Y + m\angle Z = 180°$. $m\angle Z = 180° - 110° - 100°$ — that's negative. These expressions don't form a valid triangle with $x=40$. The problem has inconsistent data. Let me provide a consistent example: $m\angle X = 2x$, $m\angle Y = 3x$, exterior at $Z = 100°$. Then $2x + 3x = 100°$ → $5x = 100°$ → $x = 20°$. $m\angle X = 40°$, $m\angle Y = 60°$, $m\angle Z = 180° - 100° = 80°$ (or $180° - 40° - 60° = 80°$). Check: $40°+60°+80°=180°$ ✓. Exterior $= 40°+60°=100°$ ✓.

image.alt: Three triangles with work shown: (1) Interior 50°, 70°, 60° with exterior 120°. (2) Exterior 130° giving interior angles 50°, 55°, 75°. (3) Algebraic solution with exterior = sum of remotes, consistent triangle verified.
image.caption: Using both theorems: finding interior and exterior angles through multiple solution paths.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) $m\angle C = 60°$, exterior at $C = 120°$.
2) Interior angles: $50°$, $55°$, $75°$.
3) $x = 20$, angles: $40°$, $60°$, $80°$ (exterior at $Z = 100°$).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem, Exterior Angle Theorem, and Linear Pair Postulate working together.

sections.section_title: Practice: Angle Sum and Exterior Angle Theorems
sections.introduction: Mastery of these fundamental theorems requires practice finding missing angles, solving algebraic equations, and applying both theorems in various configurations. Work through these problems to build fluency.

worked_examples.title: Angle Sum and Exterior Angle Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Two angles of a triangle measure $42°$ and $88°$. Find the third angle and classify the triangle by angles.
2) In $\triangle ABC$, the exterior angle at $B$ measures $115°$, and $m\angle A = 48°$. Find $m\angle C$.
3) Triangle angles are $x°$, $(x+30)°$, and $(2x-15)°$. Find all three angles.
4) An exterior angle of a triangle is $150°$ and one remote interior angle is $90°$. Is this possible? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation:
$42° + 88° + m\angle C = 180°$ → $130° + m\angle C = 180°$ → $m\angle C = 50°$.

Angles: $42°$, $50°$, $88°$ — all $< 90°$ → acute triangle.

Exterior at $B = 115°$ = $m\angle A + m\angle C$ (remote interiors).

$48° + m\angle C = 115°$ → $m\angle C = 67°$.

Then $m\angle B = 180° - 115° = 65°$ (linear pair). Check: $48°+65°+67°=180°$ ✓.

Exterior $= 150°$, one remote $= 90°$.

By Exterior Angle Theorem: $150° = 90° +$ other remote → other remote $= 60°$.

Adjacent interior $= 180° - 150° = 30°$.

Interior angles: $30°$, $60°$, $90°$. Sum $= 180°$ ✓. This IS possible — it's a 30°-60°-90° right triangle. The exterior angle of $150°$ is at the vertex of the $30°$ angle.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Acute triangle with angles 42°, 50°, 88°. (2) Triangle with exterior 115°, interior angles solved. (3) Algebraic triangle with x=41.25. (4) 30-60-90 right triangle with exterior 150° shown.
image.caption: Angle Sum and Exterior Angle practice: finding missing angles, algebra, and possibility checks.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Third angle = 50°; acute triangle.
2) $m\angle C = 67°$.
3) $41.25°$, $71.25°$, $67.5°$.
4) Yes, possible — it's a 30°-60°-90° right triangle.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem and Exterior Angle Theorem for finding angles, solving equations, and verifying triangle possibility.

summary: The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem and Exterior Angle Theorem are foundational results in geometry. Key points: 1) Triangle Angle Sum Theorem: $m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180°$ for every triangle. Used to find a missing angle when two are known, or to set up algebraic equations when angles involve variables. 2) Exterior Angle Theorem: $m\angle_{\text{exterior}} = m\angle_{\text{remote1}} + m\angle_{\text{remote2}}$. The exterior angle (formed by extending a side) equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles. 3) Exterior Angle Inequality: The exterior angle is greater than either remote interior angle individually (but not necessarily greater than the adjacent interior angle). 4) Connection: The Exterior Angle Theorem is proved from the Angle Sum Theorem and the Linear Pair Postulate. Both can be used to solve problems—choose the most efficient path. 5) Applications: Finding unknown angles, solving for variables, classifying triangles, and verifying the consistency of given triangle data. 6) These theorems are used throughout geometry: in congruence proofs, similarity, trigonometry, and polygon angle sums.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Triangle Angle Theorems' in center. Two main branches: Angle Sum Theorem ($A+B+C=180°$, proved via parallel lines, applies to all triangles) and Exterior Angle Theorem (exterior = sum of two remotes, proved from Angle Sum + Linear Pair, exterior > each remote). Sub-branches: Applications (find missing angles, algebra, classification), Connections (linear pair, polygon angle sums).
review_questions_image.caption: Angle Sum Theorem and Exterior Angle Theorem Review: the two foundational angle theorems for all triangles.
